The Subjunctive Mood (Η Υποτακτική)

Welcome to a uniquely Greek problem.

In English, we have something called the infinitive. It's the base form of a verb, usually starting with "to". - I want to go. - We need to sleep. - They can to run (wait, we say "they can run", but you get the idea).

Here is the problem: Greek does not have an infinitive.

There is no word for "to go". There is only "I go", "You go", "He goes".

So how do you translate a sentence like "I want to go"?

1. The Magic Word: Να

Because Greek verbs are always conjugated (they always have an ending pointing to a person), Greek solves the "to go" problem by using the little word να (na).

Να acts like the English word "to". But instead of being followed by a base verb, it is followed by a fully conjugated verb!

Let's look at the literal translation: - English: I want to go. - Greek (Literal): I want that I go. - Greek (Actual): Θέλω να πάω.

Let's try another one: - English: Do you want to play? - Greek (Literal): Do you want that you play? - Greek (Actual): Θέλεις να παίζεις;

Notice what happened there? Both verbs have to match the person doing the action! If I want, and I am the one going, then BOTH verbs must end in -ω: (Θέλω να πάω). If You want, and You are the one playing, BOTH verbs must end in -εις: (Θέλεις να παίζεις;).

This is called the Subjunctive Mood.

2. When to Use "Να"

You will use να constantly. Anytime you have two verbs stacked next to each other in English, you will almost certainly need a να separating them in Greek.

Here are the most common verbs that trigger the Subjunctive: - Θέλω να... (I want to...) - Μπορώ να... (I can...) - Πρέπει να... (I must...) - Ξέρω να... (I know how to...)

Μπορούμε να κάνουμε κάτι; (Can we do something?) Notice the matching endings: Bορούμε and κάνουμε.

Ξέρει να μιλάει αγγλικά. (He knows how to speak English.) Notice the matching endings: Ξέρει and μιλάει.

3. The Standalone "Na" (Commands & Wishes)

The word να is so powerful that it doesn't even need a first verb!

If you start a sentence with να, it turns the verb into a strong suggestion, a wish, or a mild command.

  • Να προσέχεις! (Take care! / Careful!)
  • Να είσαι καλά. (Be well. / The most common way to say thank you/goodbye!)
  • Να πάμε; (Shall we go?)

The Ultimate Question: Τι να κάνω;

If you are confused, lost, or asking for advice, this is the only phrase you need: Τι να κάνω; (What should I do? / literally: What that I do?).

🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist

  • [ ] I understand that Greek does not have an "infinitive" verb (like "to run").
  • [ ] I know that Greek uses να + a conjugated verb to translate things like "I want to run".
  • [ ] I know that if the "want-er" and the "run-er" are the same person, both verbs must have the exact same ending (Θέλω να τρέχω).
